_doc: Targeted text corrections applied to raw wikisource text during transform. Keys are 'vol:page' (zero-padded vol, ws page number). Each entry is a list of {from, to} replacements applied literally to raw_text before any other processing. Use sparingly — for transcription typos only, not for rendering issues.
_2:6_note: Vol 2 contributor table mistypes Grant Showerman's signature initials as 'G. Sh.' All other volumes (07, 12, 18, 26) and vol 29's Index of Contributors use 'G. Sn.' — the canonical signature. Without this fix the contributor-table builder creates a phantom 'G. Sh.' Contributor record with no articles attached.
_6:680_note: Vol 6 page 680 article footer (one of the LOUIS XIV-era articles) signs James Thomson Shotwell as 'J. T. S.' (no asterisk).  His canonical signature throughout the corpus is 'J. T. S.*' (per all 12 per-volume contributor tables, all 13 other body-footer signatures, and vol 29's Index of Contributors).  Without this fix the bare '(J. T. S.)' has no matching ContributorInitials row and Shotwell loses attribution for this article.

6:680.from: {{EB1911 footer initials|James Thomson Shotwell|J. T. S.}}
6:680.to: {{EB1911 footer initials|James Thomson Shotwell|J. T. S.*}}

_8:12_note: Vol 8 contributor table mistypes Thomas Frederick Tout's signature initials as 'T. F. T' (missing trailing period). Without the period, the contributor record's initials key doesn't match the body footers' '(T. F. T.)' signatures, so EDWARD I, EDWARD II, EDWARD III, and EDWARD THE BLACK PRINCE all silently lose Tout's author attribution. Vol 29's Index of Contributors confirms the canonical signature is 'T. F. T.' with period.

8:12.from:
| initials     = T. F. T
 | name         = [[Author:Thomas Frederick Tout
8:12.to:
| initials     = T. F. T.
 | name         = [[Author:Thomas Frederick Tout

_9:11_note: Vol 9 contributor table mistypes William Bacher's signature initials as 'W. B*.' (asterisk in wrong position). All other volumes (01, 11, 13, 15, 22, 24) and vol 29's Index of Contributors use the canonical 'W. Ba.' (subscript-a form). Without this fix the asterisk-normalization rule '*.' → '.*' would collapse W. B*. into W. B.* (William Burton's signature) — distinct people accidentally merged.
_12:734_note: Vol 12 page 734 GUIZOT, FRANÇOIS PIERRE GUILLAUME footer is co-authored by Henry Reeve and James Thomson Shotwell, but Shotwell's secondary-author signature is typed bare 'J. T. S.' (asterisk dropped).  Same fix as vol 6:680 — Shotwell's canonical signature is 'J. T. S.*' and the bare form has no matching ContributorInitials row.
_12:7_note: Vol 12 contributor table mistypes Ernest Charles François Babelon's signature initials as 'E. B.' (missing asterisk).  All other volumes that list him (01:25, 05:5, 16:7, 27:7) and vol 29's Index of Contributors use the canonical 'E. B.*' — the asterisked form distinguishes him from Edward Breck, who owns the un-asterisked 'E. B.' per vol 29.  Without this fix the build script's name-based merge attaches both initials forms to Babelon (since vol 12 entry's name field links to Author:Ernest Charles François Babelon), leaving 'E. B.' permanently bound to Babelon and the Group-Y pair re-key in the linker unable to free 'E. B.' for Breck.  Confirmed via the entry's description field (Collège de France, Bibliothèque Nationale, etc.) and subject1=Hadrumetum — unambiguously Babelon's Roman-archaeology bio.

12:7.from:
| initials     = E. B.
 | name         = [[Author:Ernest Charles François Babelon
12:7.to:
| initials     = E. B.*
 | name         = [[Author:Ernest Charles François Babelon

_17:10_note: Vol 17 contributor table lists John Theodore Merz at 'J. T. M.' (no asterisk) — same key James Tayler Milton uses in vol 04:10.  This is a print-level ambiguity: the original print never disambiguates the two J. T. M. contributors.  We assign asterisk to Merz (alphabetically first per the Babelon/Breck convention; see project_vol29_contributor_integration.md) so vol 04:10's Milton retains the un-asterisked 'J. T. M.' and Merz claims 'J. T. M.*'.  This is editorial, not a transcription typo — the print agrees with the wikitext.  Pair with the vol 17:40 LOTZE-footer correction so Merz's body-footer signature also resolves.

17:10.from:
| initials    = J. T. M.
 | name        = [[Author:John Theodore Merz
17:10.to:
| initials    = J. T. M.*
 | name        = [[Author:John Theodore Merz

_17:40_note: Vol 17 page 40 LOTZE article footer co-authors John Theodore Merz and Henry Sturt; Merz's signature is plain 'J. T. M.' (matching the print's print-level ambiguity with James Tayler Milton).  Pair with the vol 17:10 front-matter correction — once Merz owns 'J. T. M.*' in the contributor table, this footer must use the asterisked form to bind correctly.  Sturt's 'H. St.' is unaffected.

17:40.from: {{EB1911 footer initials|John Theodore Merz|J. T. M.|name2=Henry Sturt|initials2=H. St.}}
17:40.to: {{EB1911 footer initials|John Theodore Merz|J. T. M.*|name2=Henry Sturt|initials2=H. St.}}

_4:162_note: Vol 4 page 162 BOILER Fig. 17 caption transcribes 'Bobcock & Wilcox' — a typo for the correct 'Babcock & Wilcox' (the firm's name appears correctly elsewhere in the same article).  Source-text typo, not a rendering issue.
_26:1093_note: Vol 26 page 1093 section s3 transcribes the article heading as '''LEO TOLSTOY''' — given-name-first.  The print original (and the page running-header template `{{x-larger|{{uc|TOLL—TOLSTOY, L.}}}}` on the same page) uses surname-first 'TOLSTOY, LEO', the EB1911 convention for biographical articles.  Without this fix our boundary detector picks up 'LEO TOLSTOY' as the title; the article then loses its match against vol 29's Index entry 'Tolstoy, Leo' (Charles Theodore Hagberg Wright), leaving Hagberg Wright orphaned.
_20:13_note: Vol 20 contributor table writes John Charles Van Dyke's signature with lowercase 'van' in both the initials (J. C. van D.) and the Author-link slug — disagreeing with vol 14's canonical capital-V form (J. C. Van D., Author:John Charles Van Dyke).  Net effect: build_contributor_table.py creates two distinct Contributor records for one person; vol 14's contribution (INNESS, GEORGE) goes to one record, vol 20's (PAINTING, U.S.) to the other.  Capital V is the standard reference form for the American art historian (1856-1932).

20:13.from:
| initials     = J. C. van D.
 | name         = [[Author:John Charles van Dyke|John Charles van Dyke]]
20:13.to:
| initials     = J. C. Van D.
 | name         = [[Author:John Charles Van Dyke|John Charles Van Dyke]]

_20:569_note: Vol 20 page 569 PAINTING article footer uses lowercase initials (J. C. van D.) inconsistent with the canonical J. C. Van D. used in vol 14's footers and matching vol 14's Author-link slug.  Same single-record vs duplicate-records issue as vol 20:13.

20:569.from: {{EB1911 footer initials|John Charles Van Dyke|J. C. van D.}}
20:569.to: {{EB1911 footer initials|John Charles Van Dyke|J. C. Van D.}}

_21:7_note: Vol 21 contributor table mistypes Rev. Ethelred Luke Taunton's signature initials as 'E. Tu.'  All other volumes (02, 03, 05, 07, 10, 15, 17, 27) use 'E. Tn.' — the correct signature per article footers and the vol 29 Index of Contributors. Without this fix the contributor-table builder creates a phantom 'E. Tu.' Contributor record with no articles attached.
_27:10_note: Vol 27 contributor table mistypes Rev. John Punnett Peters' signature initials as 'J. P. P.' — the entry is otherwise unambiguously Peters (description names Episcopal Cathedral / Hebrew at Penn / Nippur Expedition; subject1 = Ur). All other volumes (01, 03, 04, 07, 09, 13, 14, 15, 19, 26) use 'J. P. Pe.' for Peters, also confirmed by vol 29's Index of Contributors. The bug then compounds in build_contributor_table.py: the J. P. P. initials group accumulates this 1 Peters entry alongside 6 legitimate John Percival Postgate entries (vols 15, 16, 17, 21, 22, 26); the 'longest canonical name wins' heuristic picks 'Rev. John Punnett Peters' over 'John Percival Postgate'; both J. P. P. and J. P. Pe. groups then merge under Peters; Postgate vanishes from the DB entirely; and Postgate's articles (Juvenal, Latin Literature, Lucan, Phaedrus, Propertius, Textual Criticism, Tibullus) get wrongly credited to Peters.

27:10.from:
| initials     = J. P. P.
 | name         = [[Author:John Punnett Peters
27:10.to:
| initials     = J. P. Pe.
 | name         = [[Author:John Punnett Peters

26:244.from: The secretary y had already accepted a bribe
26:244.to: The secretary had already accepted a bribe

_4:682_note: Wikisource transcription typo: '''BROWN. JOHN''' (period after BROWN) — should be a comma. Causes the boundary detector to take only 'BROWN' as the title since the period looks like sentence-end. Article is John Brown of Haddington (1722-1787), Scottish divine.

_2:567_note: ARITHMETIC § 28 uses [[File:Sinnbild Autokreuzung.svg|12px]] as an inline math glyph for 'reverse multiplication'. We have no inline-image renderer; substitute the Unicode vector-cross-product character ⨯ (U+2A2F) which is visually similar and distinct from × (used elsewhere in the same paragraph for normal multiplication).
_27:408_note: TUNICATA Fig. 32 has a complex outer-table containing a rowspan'd inner legend that the layout extractor can't parse — the Fig. 32 caption is lost and the inner table renders as HTMLTABLE with loose pipes. Split the outer (image+attribution+caption) from the inner legend (rewritten as a flat 2-column legend, no rowspan) so each goes through its own clean handler.

_2:579_note: ARITHMETIC Length-units table has a transcription typo: '{{EB1911 tfrac|10' missing its closing }} before the next || cell separator, causing the cell-parsing to swallow the next cell.
_27:30_note: TOOL page 30 is unproofed OCR (wikisource quality level 1). Figs 4-7 are on Commons? NO — Figs 4, 5, 6, 7 are NOT uploaded as individual crops (gap in the 66-image TOOL set on Commons). Instead, use the full-page DjVu raster {{raw image|...djvu/30}} once at the top of the figure region, with all four captions+legends stacked beneath it. Also fix two body-prose splits caused by image intrusion: 'straightE'/'forward' (should be 'straightforward') and 'But in all other'/'respects'. Fractions like '⅛ in.' for OCR-misread '%' glyphs. 'er' typo in 'Narrow cross-cut er cape' → 'or'.

27:30.to:
B is of straightforward type, used for turning, planing and shaping. As the correct tool angle can only occur on the middle plane of the tool, it is usual to employ cranked tools, C, D, E, right- and left-handed, for heavy and moderately heavy duty, the direction of the cranking corresponding with that in which the tool is required to traverse. Tools for boring are cranked and many for planing (fig. 3). The slotting tools (fig. 5) embody the same principle, but their shanks are in line with the direction of cutting. Many roughing and finishing tools are of knife type H. Finishing tools have broad edges, F, G, H. They occur in straightforward and right- and left-hand types.

[[File:EB1911 - Tool - Fig 6.png|Fig. 6.—Group of Chisels.]]

A, Paring chisel. B, Socket chisel for heavy duty. C, Common chipping chisel. D, Narrow cross-cut or cape chisel. E, Cow-mouth chisel, or gouge. F, Straight chisel or sett. G, Hollow chisel or sett.

_26:428_note: Vol 26 page 428 TAPESTRY plate page contains a transcription typo in a Ts styling template: `{{ts|p0}|ac}} colspan="3"|` — the close brace is misplaced (a stray `}` after p0, breaking the template into `{{ts|p0}` plus orphan `|ac}}`). Should be `{{ts|p0|ac}}` (single Ts template with two style classes). Without the fix the malformed template + bare `colspan="3"|` leaks into the rendered body. Single occurrence corpus-wide.
_14:738_note: INTERPOLATION uses wiki `|width=5%|(N).` cells for equation-number annotations IMMEDIATELY after an HTML ``, with no `|-` row separator between them. Without the row separator the outer wiki table's parser doesn't recognise the wiki cell as a new row, and the `|width=5%|(N).` leaks into body text as literal characters. Insert `|-` rows so each equation-number cell sits in its own row of the outer wiki table.
_14:739_note: Same INTERPOLATION pattern as 14:738 — equation-number wiki cell directly after `` needs a `|-` row separator.
_18:302_note: METEOROLOGY equation (1) was transcribed as italic text `''pv''=RT` instead of `<math>pv = RT</math>`. The {{ne}} numbered-equation handler correctly renders math content as a labeled display equation with the (N) at the right margin, but italic-text content falls through to inline rendering and the label sticks to the formula. Rewrite to a real math block.
_28:403_note: Vol 28 p403 (printed p386) reference to 'Lyon v. The Fishmongers' Company' transcribes the genitive apostrophe inside the italic span as a third straight apostrophe, producing '''The Fishmongers''''.  MediaWiki parses three apostrophes as bold-toggle, which on Wikisource itself just renders the line oddly but in our converter prior to line-scoping the dangling marker cascaded through the rest of the page and ate WATER-SCORPION, WATERSHED, WATERSPOUT, and WHEAT.  Line-scoping (2026-05-16) already contains the damage to the line; this correction additionally fixes the typo by closing italic and using U+2019 for the genitive so the line renders cleanly.
_28:269_note: Vol 28 p269 (printed p253) bold heading transcribes Pierre Waldeck-Rousseau's surname as 'WALDECK-ROUSSSAU' with three S's.  The page-heading running header on the same page has the correct two-S 'WALDECK-ROUSSEAU', confirming the bold heading is a typo.  Without this fix the article title carries the misspelling.
_28:510_note: Vol 28 page 510 WEIGHTS AND MEASURES has `{{ditto|{India: }}` on the Bombay and Madras `Guz` rows — a stray `{` in the ditto template's content arg. Pipeline can't unwrap because the regex excludes braces in content. Source-side fix: drop the stray brace.
_11:582_note: GEMS PLATE II — the printed page has a centered credit beneath the plate ("All the above are in the British Museum, unless otherwise stated.") that the Wikisource transcription omitted. PLATE I has its analogous credit; restore PLATE II's so the two plates render consistently and the gem attributions are accurate.

11:582.from: {{em|3}}76. Athena, from Townley Bust by Marchant. {{EB1911 fine print/e}}
11:582.to:
{{em|3}}76. Athena, from Townley Bust by Marchant. {{EB1911 fine print/e}}
All the above are in the British Museum, unless otherwise stated.

_3:221_note: BAG-PIPE vol 3 p221 has an orphan `|}` (stray wiki-table close) on its own line between the {{img float}} for Bag-pipe music 5.png and the continuation 'the chaunter.' There's no matching `{|` open — Wikisource transcription typo. Without this fix the stray `|}` leaks into the rendered body.
_1:10_note: Editorial Preface (vol 1 print p. xi / leaf 10) — the Wikisource transcription drops the opening clause of the first sentence. Per the print scan the paragraph reads 'In the Prefatory Note the history of the production of the successive editions of the Encyclopædia Britannica has been briefly told; and elsewhere in these volumes, under the heading of Encyclopædia…' but Wikisource starts at 'Elsewhere in these volumes…' with the drop-cap on E. Patch restores the missing clause and moves the drop-cap to 'I' of 'In'. Used by tools/viewer/build_preface.py via britannica.corrections.apply_corrections.

1:10.from: {{nodent|{{dropinitial|E|4em}}{{larger|LSEWHERE}} in these volumes, under the heading of
1:10.to: {{nodent|{{dropinitial|I|4em}}{{larger|n}} the Prefatory Note the history of the production of the successive editions of the ''Encyclopædia Britannica'' has been briefly told; and elsewhere in these volumes, under the heading of
